text: The patient is positioned supine with the right arm tucked, and a bump is placed on the left side of the abdomen to elevate the spleen. The abdomen is prepped and draped in sterile fashion. The surgeon accesses the abdomen at Palmer's point in the left upper quadrant, places additional ports, and uses an Athenson liver retractor for abdominal inspection.

The surgeon incises the short gastric vessels along the upper greater curvature of the stomach using a sealing device, continuing dissection up to the angle of His. The dissection then shifts to the inferior pole of the spleen, where the splenocolic ligament is divided. The surgeon carefully mobilizes the posterior and lateral attachments from the inferior portion of the spleen, ensuring the spleen remains unharmed.

The surgeon performs dissection of loose areolar tissue using a combination of blunt dissection with a KITNER and a sealing device. With the inferior and lateral attachments divided, attention is turned to the superior pole of the spleen. Additional attachments are lysed up to the hilum. The spleen is fully mobilized, and the remaining attachments to the hilum are divided using several vascular load staple line reinforced endogia staplers, requiring two or three staple firings for complete division.

The surgeon places the fully divided spleen into a large endocatch bag and extracts it through the left lateral port. A Kelly clamp and ring forcep are used to morselize the spleen carefully, ensuring the bag remains intact to prevent spillage. A hemostatic agent is applied to the splenic bed to control bleeding.
